children.title: Contents
children.description: The section outlines UCIe terminology, reference documents, and revision history, then enumerates core components—Protocol Layer, Die‑to‑Die (D2D) Adapter, Physical Layer, Interfaces—and configuration types—Single‑Module Configuration, Multi‑module Configurations—answering what each component and configuration entails, how they interoperate, and which setups are supported.

children.title: Figures
children.description: This section outlines UCIe system architecture, detailing technical configurations for Standard, Advanced, and 3D packaging, physical layer components, and module interconnects. It answers questions regarding how UCIe scales connectivity across various package types and physical module arrangements.

children.title: Tables
children.description: Provides a concise reference index of all specification tables—Definitions (Table 1‑1), Unit Symbols (2‑1), Reference Documents (3‑1), Revision History (4‑1), Standard‑Package (1‑1), Advanced‑Package (1‑2), 3‑D‑Package (1‑3) characteristics, 2D/2.5D and 3D performance targets (1‑4 to 1‑5), Advanced‑Package bump‑pitch groups (1‑6), Spec‑to‑Protocol mode requirements (2‑1), mandatory negotiation capabilities (3‑1), and Flit Header Format 2 without Retry (3‑2)—answering where designers locate exact terminology, measurement units, version changes, package‑level electrical/mechanical specs, KPI thresholds, bump‑pitch classifications, protocol‑mode mappings, required link‑partner negotiations, and header‑format details for UCIe implementation.

children.title: Terminology
children.description: This section defines critical technical terminology for the UCIe 3.0 specification, covering nomenclature ranging from Advanced Package and chiplet architecture to signal integrity terms like AFE, BER, and bubble. It serves as a foundational reference for interpreting acronyms and industry-standard definitions essential for implementing UCIe interconnects and verifying protocol compliance.

children.title: Revision History
children.description: This section outlines the evolution of the UCIe specification from version 2.0 to 3.0, detailing key updates such as 48/64 GT/s data rates, UCIe-3D integration, and enhanced manageability protocols. It serves as a reference document for identifying specific version changes, supported protocol revisions, and regulatory technical standards.

children.title: 1.0 Introduction
children.description: This section introduces the UCIe 1.0 architecture as an open, multi-protocol on-package interconnect designed for disaggregated die systems. It defines the standard’s scope—including the physical layer, link layer, application protocols (PCIe, CXL, Streaming), manageability infrastructure, and DFx (design-for-test/debug) architecture—to clarify how UCIe achieves cross-vendor interoperability in chiplet-based designs.

children.title: 1.1 UCIe Components
children.description: This section details the architectural hierarchy of the UCIe specification, covering the Protocol Layer, D2D Adapter, Physical Layer, and interface definitions. It defines how data moves across chiplet boundaries, addressing fundamental questions regarding stack modularity, link training, and physical interconnect standardization.

children.title: 1.1.1 Protocol Layer
children.description: UCIe Protocol Layer overview: layered architecture, component functionality, timeout/error specifications, supported protocols (PCIe, CXL – excluding

children.title: 1.1.3 Physical Layer
children.description: This section explains the UCIe Physical Layer architecture—including Modules, Lanes, AFE implementation, and lane‑count specifications for Standard and Advanced Packages—and describes how the Physical Layer interacts with the D2D Adapter and Protocol Layer to manage data path organization, link bring‑up, and power‑management coordination, answering what the structural granularity of the UCIe data path is, how lane organization is defined, and how the Physical Layer supports protocol and reliability features.

children.title: 1.1.4 Interfaces
children.description: Routing description: This section details the UCIe 1.1.4 Interfaces, covering the Raw D2D Interface between Physical Layer

children.title: 1.2 UCIe Configurations
children.description: This section defines the structural architecture of UCIe interconnects by detailing single-module deployment, multi-module scaling, and sideband-only operational modes. It addresses physical configuration requirements, link-width scaling, and management interface protocols necessary for heterogenous chiplet integration.

children.title: 1.2.1 Single Module Configuration
children.description: This section outlines single module configuration parameters, including Standard Package pin counts (x16/x8) and Logical Physical Layer sequencing responsibilities for link management. It defines the hierarchical architecture—Physical Layer, D2D Adapter, and Protocol Layer—establishing the interoperability standards via the Raw D2D and Flit-aware D2D interfaces.

children.title: 1.2.2 Multi-module Configurations
children.description: This section outlines requirements for two-module and four-module UCIe configurations, specifying that combined modules must maintain synchronized data rates and widths. It details architectural implementations—including Multi-Module PHY Logic, Die-to-Die Adapters, and Electrical/AFE interfaces—to define how standardized multi-module layouts are structured.

children.title: 1.2.3 Sideband-only Configurations
children.description: Sideband‑only configurations — one‑, two‑, and four‑port sideband‑only links, x64/x32 sideband lanes, Electrical/AFE interfaces, PHY logic, die‑to‑die adapters, valid‑track and FW‑CLK signaling—are defined for test and manageability, detailing how sideband‑only links are architected, validated, and integrated with UCIe Retimers for off‑package interconnect extensions. This section answers which sideband‑only topologies are supported, the required electrical/AFE and PHY resources, and how they connect to retimers to enable extended channel reach.

children.title: 1.3 UCIe Retimers
children.description: Section 1.3 details UCIe Retimer architecture, off‑package interconnect extension, link topology (UCIe Link 0/1, remote Retimer partner), error‑handling mechanisms (FEC, CRC, BER, error correlation) and protocol compatibility (PCIe, CXL), answering how retimers reliably transfer flits, configure UCIe links, and conform to underlying specification error models.

children.title: 1.4 UCIe Key Performance Targets
children.description: This section outlines UCIe 2.0 and 2.5D performance benchmarks, focusing on die edge bandwidth density (GB/s per mm) and energy efficiency (pJ/bit) across scaling link speeds from 4 GT/s to 64 GT/s. It defines comparative metrics for Advanced versus Standard Package configurations to address technical requirements for chiplet interconnect scalability and power consumption.

children.title: 1.5 Interoperability
children.description: Section 1.5 details UCIe interoperability requirements, addressing package interconnects, voltage levels, bump pitch scaling, and performance trade-offs for Advanced and Standard packaging. It defines how data rates and physical dimensions ensure functional compatibility between disparate die implementations and process nodes.

children.title: 2.0 Protocol Layer
children.description: UCIe 2.0 Protocol Layer – mapping of PCIe (Flit mode & non‑Flit mode) and CXL (68 B Flit mode, 256 B Flit mode) onto UCIe mainband, definition of Transaction Layer, Link Layer (flow‑control negotiation, retry‑free transport), and separation of protocol features from Flit format/packetization. Answers: which protocol layers and transport mechanisms UCIe supports, how PCIe and CXL protocols are negotiated and mapped, and which protocol‑level features are independent of flit packing.

children.title: 2.1 PCIe
children.description: This section details PCIe flit architecture, covering technical specifications for 68B and 256B formats, header structures, and latency-optimized configurations. It addresses how PCIe data link layer packets are encapsulated, formatted, and transmitted to ensure protocol efficiency and compatibility across various flit types.

children.title: 2.1.4 Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format
children.description: This section outlines the Standard 256B Start Header Flit format within the UCIe 3.0 specification, defining the operational transport mechanisms for PCIe protocol integration. It clarifies standard flit formats, clarifies support for Raw versus Flit Mode configurations, and details requirements for PCIe-based UCIe Retimers.

children.title: 2.1.5 Latency-Optimized 256B with Optional Bytes Flit Format
children.description: This section details the Latency‑Optimized 256‑byte Optional‑Bytes Flit format for PCIe Flit Mode in UCIe Spec 3.0, covering Flit_Marker assignment, DLP‑byte driving, reserved‑field handling, omission of PM/Link‑Management DLLPs, and recommended elimination of 8b/10b, 128b/130b, and non‑Flit CRC/Retry framing logic; it answers how to implement the format, which protocol fields are required or reserved, and which legacy mechanisms can be omitted for area‑ and power‑efficient designs.

children.title: 2.2 CXL 256B Flit Mode
children.description: This section details the architectural specification of CXL 256B Flit mode, covering raw data encodings, latency-optimized formats, and standard start header structures. It defines the physical layer framing and protocol-specific header requirements essential for implementing high-bandwidth, low-latency CXL data transport mechanisms.

children.title: 2.2.2 Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats
children.description: This section details Latency-Optimized 256B Flit formats for UCIe, specifying requirements for PCIe Protocol Layer integration, FDI Flit transfer, and the exclusion of legacy 8b/10b, 128b/130b, and non-Flit framing logic. It clarifies how to optimize chiplet area and power by defining protocol-specific DLP byte handling and the omission of non-applicable DLLPs in Die-to-Die Adapter communications.

children.title: 2.2.3 Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format
children.description: Section 2.2.3 defines the mandatory **Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format** for **CXL.io and CXL.cachemem** protocols over **UCIe**, detailing **FDI** interface requirements, **DLLP** handling, and **Viral containment** mechanisms. It addresses technical specifications for interoperability, field registration, and the exclusion of specific CXL link layer packets within the 256B Flit mode architecture.

children.title: 2.3 CXL 68B Flit Mode
children.description: This section details CXL 68B Flit Mode protocols, covering raw bit-stream mapping, slot signaling, and packet framing configurations. It addresses how the Physical Layer organizes data for 68-byte flit transfers, defining field placement and overhead management requirements for high-bandwidth CXL link initialization.

children.title: 2.3.2 68B Flit Format
children.description: UCIe 3.0 §2.3.2 covers the 68‑bit Flit format for

children.title: 2.4 Streaming Protocol
children.description: This section covers the Ultra Accelerator Link (UAL) streaming protocol, detailing data encapsulation methods including Raw, 68B, and 256B standard or latency-optimized flit formats. It defines packet structure, framing overhead, and throughput efficiency, answering how data is serialized and mapped onto physical interconnects for low-latency transmission.

children.title: 2.4.2 68B Flit Format
children.description: This section defines the 68B Flit format requirements for CXL.io and CXL.cachemem protocols within the UCIe 1.0 architecture. It specifies handling of Framing, DLLPs, LCRC, Retry mechanisms, and Link Initialization, clarifying how Protocol Layer operations are bypassed or modified when relying on the UCIe Adapter for reliable transport.

children.title: 2.4.4 Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats
children.description: This section defines **Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats** within the **UCIe Streaming Flit protocol**, detailing the architectural requirements for **FDI (Flit-to-Die Interconnect)** data handling. It addresses how the **Die-to-Die Adapter** manages flit header insertion, CRC calculation, and byte alignment to minimize latency for high-performance chiplet communication.

children.title: 2.5 Management Transport Protocol
children.description: This section details UCIe Management Transport Protocols, defining Protocol Layer data delivery via FDI, Flit header/CRC insertion, and byte-shifting requirements for Standard and Latency-Optimized 256B Flit formats. It specifies how these transport mechanisms handle data encapsulation, framing overhead, and receiver-side bit masking for streaming-capable chiplet interconnects.

children.title: 3.0 Die-to-Die Adapter
children.description: The Die‑to‑Die Adapter section details reliable data transfer (CRC/Retry or parity), arbitration / muxing for multiple Protocol Layers, link‑state management, and remote protocol‑parameter negotiation, describing the FDI (Flit‑aware Die‑to‑Die) and RDI (raw Die‑to‑Die) interfaces, PHY logic, electrical/AFE slice allocation, and sideband/global signaling; it answers how the adapter ensures data integrity, coordinates multi‑protocol arbitration, negotiates link parameters, and maps protocol‑layer functions onto physical‑layer resources.

children.title: 3.1 Stack Multiplexing
children.description: This section outlines the requirements for **UCIe Stack Multiplexing**, where two identical protocol stacks share a single physical Link via the **Multi_Protocol_Enable** negotiation. It defines technical constraints for bandwidth allocation, **Management Port Gateway (MPG) mux** consistency, and the mandatory **flit-interleaving** rule ensuring non-consecutive transmission from a single stack.

children.title: 3.2 Link Initialization
children.description: Section 3.2 details the Link Initialization process, focusing specifically on Adapter Initialization protocols and hardware-level handshake configurations. It addresses how physical interfaces establish communication, authenticate parameters, and transition through startup stages to reach operational readiness.

children.title: 3.2.1 Stage 3 of Link Initialization: Adapter Initialization
children.description: This section details the Adapter Initialization phase of link establishment, covering local capability discovery, parameter negotiation with remote link partners, and Fabric Direct Interface (FDI) activation. It addresses how hardware controllers synchronize operational settings and negotiate communication parameters to ensure successful link connectivity.

children.title: 3.2.1.1 Part 1: Determine Local Capabilities
children.description: This section outlines the initial phase of UCIe Adapter initialization, detailing the evaluation of Physical Layer training outcomes and Retry requirements following Stage 2 mainband training. It addresses how the Adapter assesses link-specific variables to verify configuration readiness before proceeding to parameter exchanges.

children.title: 3.2.1.2 Part 2: Parameter Exchange with Remote Link Partner
children.description: This section details the Parameter Exchange with a remote UCIe link partner, specifying negotiation of capabilities—including Raw Format, Streaming Flit Format, Enhanced Multi‑Protocol, and Retimer Receiver Buffer credits (256 B per credit)—and the generation of the {AdvCap.Adapter} sideband message based on UCIe Link Control register bits and firmware policies. It answers how adapters determine which capability bits to advertise, under what conditions Raw Format must be set, and how Retimer buffer credits are calculated for Mainband data storage.

children.title: 3.2.1.3 Part 3: FDI bring up
children.description: This section details the FDI bring-up sequence and critical 8-ms timeout requirements for UCIe Parameter Exchange completion. It establishes protocol-layer transition mandates, RDI state management, and Retimer stall-message requirements to prevent UIE-level LinkError states during initialization.

children.title: 3.3 Operation Formats
children.description: This section defines link-layer operation formats, including raw, 68B, and 256B flit structures, while outlining protocol-layer implementation requirements. It addresses how data is encapsulated for transmission and specifies the architectural constraints governing flit assembly and adapter design across diverse protocols.

children.title: 3.3.2.1 68B Flit Format Alignment and Padding Rules
children.description: Section 3.3.2.1 explains 68‑byte flit format alignment, D2D‑Adapter‑induced 4‑byte shift, 256‑byte transfer granularity, Pause‑of‑Data‑Stream (PDS) Flit Header, 0‑padding to 64‑byte boundaries, additional all‑zero 64‑byte chunks for 256‑byte alignment, and receiver byte‑shifter reset, answering how alignment, padding, and power‑saving pause mechanisms are implemented and excluded from the protocol layer in UCIe links.

children.title: 3.3.3 Standard 256B Flit Formats
children.description: Section 3.3.3 defines Standard 256B Flit Formats (Format 3 and Format 4) for PCIe and CXL, detailing the structural mapping of protocol headers, payload data, and cyclic redundancy checks (CRC). It addresses how these flits are formatted and padded for compatibility within the UCIe Die-to-Die Adapter architecture.

children.title: 3.3.4 Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats
children.description: This section details the architectural implementation of UCIe Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats 5 and 6, focusing on the inclusion of optional protocol bytes versus standard configurations. It addresses essential questions regarding CXL, PCIe, and Streaming protocol compatibility, header-bit requirements, and the technical distinction between flit formats optimized for reduced transmission latency.

children.title: 3.3.5 Flit Format-related Implementation Requirements for Protocol Layer and Adapter
children.description: Routing: explains UCIe Flit formats (Raw, 68‑byte, 256‑byte End Header) and adapter

children.title: 3.4 Decision Table for Flit Format and Protocol
children.description: Defines Decision Table (Table 3‑8) and Protocol Mapping (Table 3‑7) for selecting PCIe, CXL 68B, CXL 256B, or Streaming flit formats, outlines mandatory and optional implementation requirements for Die‑to‑Die adapters, and explains how protocol‑flit negotiation during link bring‑up governs reset‑state transitions and error handling.

children.title: 3.5 State Machine Hierarchy
children.description: This section defines the UCIe hierarchical state machine architecture, detailing the functional partitioning between the Adapter Layer State Machine (LSM), RDI, and FDI to manage and coordinate link states. It clarifies protocol-specific state management for CXL, PCIe, and Streaming, answering how Link states are synchronized between remote partners and abstracted across multi-module PHY configurations.

children.title: 3.7 CRC Computation
children.description: This section specifies the 16-bit CRC polynomial ($x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$) and computation requirements for UCIe Die-to-Die Adapters. It defines the message padding, zero-extension, and LFSR initialization procedures necessary to ensure accurate error detection over 128-byte flit structures.

children.title: 3.9 Runtime Link Testing using Parity
children.description: This section details the UCIe runtime link health monitoring mechanism, specifying parity injection algorithms, calculation intervals based on configured `N` values, and data byte counting requirements. It addresses how the Adapter detects link errors by defining the XOR-based bitwise parity computation, the periodic insertion cadence of parity bytes, and the exclusion of these bytes from data stream counts.

children.title: 4.1 Data and Sideband Transmission Flow
children.description: This section details data serialization protocols, including byte-to-lane mapping, framing integrity, clock gating, and sideband signaling. It answers technical questions regarding link synchronization, efficient power management, and the architectural flow of information across physical lanes.

children.title: 4.1.1 Byte to Lane Mapping
children.description: This section defines the Logical PHY's byte-to-lane mapping protocol, detailing how data flits are distributed across parallel lanes in x16, x32, and x64 interface widths. It specifies bit-order serialization and lane-assignment procedures, providing the technical framework for maintaining proper data alignment during physical layer transmission.

children.title: 4.1.2 Valid Framing
children.description: Section 4.1.2 defines the **Valid signal framing** requirements for UCIe physical layer data transfers, specifying the mandatory 4 UI assertion/de-assertion cycle per 8-bit packet. It establishes rules for **8-UI block alignment** and synchronization between Transmitters and Receivers during the **Active state**, providing constraints for data throughput and Retimer-specific credit handling.

children.title: 4.1.4 Free Running Clock Mode
children.description: This section specifies UCIe Retimer framing requirements, including credit tracking, valid framing encodings for error detection (single-bit correction vs. triple-bit detection), and clock gating protocols. It addresses implementation rules for operating speeds above or below 48 GT/s and defines postamble requirements for forwarded mainband clocks.

children.title: 4.1.5 Sideband transmission
children.description: This section details protocols for sideband data exchange, covering Performant Mode Operation (PMO) and priority-based packet transmission. It defines technical specifications for efficient out-of-band communication, addressing how systems manage low-latency packet routing and optimized channel performance.

children.title: 4.1.5.1 Sideband Performant Mode Operation (PMO)
children.description: This section details the Sideband Performant Mode Operation (PMO) in the UCIe 3.0 specification, focusing on the Sideband Feature Extensions (SBFE) negotiation mechanism and MBINIT.PARAM requirements. It addresses how links establish high-performance capability on both transmit and receive paths to optimize sideband communication.

children.title: 4.1.5.2 Priority Sideband Packet Transfer
children.description: This section defines the architectural requirements and protocols for Priority Sideband Packet Transfer (PSPT), focusing on message ordering, bandwidth allocation, and arbitration rules for high-priority signaling. It addresses how systems manage asynchronous traffic flow and latency constraints within the sideband communication interface.

children.title: 4.1.5.2.1 PSPT Rules
children.description: This section defines the Priority Sideband Packet Transfer (PSPT) capability, detailing mandatory PMO support, negotiation procedures using `{MBINIT.PARAM SBFE}` messages, and multi-module synchronization requirements. It specifies the technical rules for enabling PSPT hardware, configuring Link Control registers, and ensuring compatibility for both transmission and reception directions.

children.title: 4.2 Lane Reversal
children.description: Lane‑reversal in UCIe 3.0: sideband transmitter interrupt timing (8‑UI multiples), idle insertion before priority traffic, opcode definitions (11111b = end‑of‑chain, 11110b = chain continuation), chaining of multiple priority packets, idle‑link transmission rules, receiver state‑tracking for interrupt handling; answers how to insert priority packets within normal traffic, when and how to use each opcode, how to chain packets without idle, and how receivers detect and manage interruptions.

children.title: 4.2.1 Lane ID
children.description: This section details UCIe lane‑reversal requirements, defining physical versus logical lanes (RCKP_P, RCKN_P, RTRK_P, TV

children.title: 4.3 Interconnect redundancy remapping
children.description: This section details interconnect redundancy mechanisms, covering data lane repair, lane reversal, and clock/track remapping for standard package interfaces. It addresses how to implement fault-tolerant signal routing, execute physical layer repairs, and manage interface width degradation to ensure architectural reliability.

children.title: 4.3.1 Data Lane repair
children.description: Data Lane repair in UCIe 3.0 details redundancy remapping for up to two faulty lanes per 32‑lane group, covering TD_P/RD_P lane groups, TRD_P/RRD_P redundant lanes, shift‑left/shift‑right multiplexing, tri‑state transmitter and disabled receiver handling, and applicability limits for x32 Advanced Package links. It answers how lane groups are partitioned, which redundant lanes map to each group, the exact shift operations for fault recovery, and the post‑repair configuration requirements.

children.title: 4.3.2 Data Lane repair with Lane reversal
4.3.3 Data Lane repair implementation
children.description: This section details the hardware-level implementation of Data Lane repair, covering fault-tolerant configurations for single and multi-lane systems including lane reversal logic. It addresses how to maintain link integrity and bandwidth availability when physical lanes experience post-fabrication failures or connectivity inversions.

children.title: 4.3.3.1 Single Lane repair
children.description: The section details the single‑lane repair mechanism for UCIe links, specifying the use of redundant lanes TRD_P[0]/RRD_P[0] (and TRD_P[2]/RRD_P[2] for the upper half of 64‑lane x64 modules) to remap any failed physical lane, the pseudo‑code algorithm for shifting TD_P and RD_P mappings (0‑31 and 32‑63 ranges), and the impact of lane reversal on mapping; it answers how a single‑lane fault is detected and corrected, which redundant lane is employed, how the re‑mapping sequence operates for x64 versus x32 Advanced Package modules, and how lane reversal modifies the physical‑to‑logical lane relationship.

children.title: 4.3.3.2 Two Lane repair
children.description: This section defines the mandatory repair protocol for two-lane physical failures within UCIe 32-lane groupings using redundant bumps TRD/RRD. It specifies the logical-to-physical remapping logic and provides pseudo-code to resolve faults in the lower (0–31) and upper (32–63) lane banks.

children.title: 4.3.3.3 Single Lane repair with Lane reversal
children.description: This section details the implementation of single-lane repair and lane reversal mechanisms using x64 and x32 advanced package pseudo-codes. It addresses core technical questions regarding memory alignment, bus-lane link training, and bitstream remapping required for hardware fault recovery.

children.title: 4.3.3.3.2 x32 Advanced Package Pseudo Codes
children.description: Routing description: This section details x32 and x64 advanced package pseudo‑code algorithms for single‑lane and two‑lane repair

children.title: 4.3.3.4 Two Lane repair with Lane reversal
children.description: This section details the logic and implementation for repairing two-lane configurations using lane reversal through x64 and x32 advanced package pseudo-codes. It addresses technical requirements for data path correction, signal integrity, and hardware-level lane mapping protocols to resolve communication failures.

children.title: 4.3.3.4.1 x64 Advanced Package Pseudo Codes
children.description: This section details the hardware-level bit-mapping logic for two-lane failure recovery within x64 UCIe Advanced Packages, utilizing specific signal-rerouting algorithms for lane reversal. It defines the procedural shifts between physical layer (TD_P/RD_P) and logical layer (TD_L/RD_L) interfaces to maintain link integrity when dual lane faults occur in either the lower or upper halves of the 64-bit data path.

children.title: 4.3.3.4.2 x32 Advanced Package Pseudo Codes
children.description: This section details the redundancy logic for x32 Advanced Package Link repair, specifically outlining lane remapping pseudo-code for dual-lane physical layer failures. It addresses how the UCIe architecture maintains link integrity through systematic reconfiguration of data, clock, and track signals when hardware defects are detected.

children.title: 4.3.4 Clock and Track Lane remapping
children.description: This section defines the hardware-level Clock and Track Lane remapping mechanisms in the UCIe 3.0 specification, detailing repair protocols for single-lane failures in differential and pseudo-differential clock/track signals. It provides technical pseudo-code and schematics to answer how physical lane sparing, transmitter tri-stating, and receiver disabling are orchestrated to preserve link integrity following a link fault.

children.title: 4.3.5 Clock and Track Lane repair implementation
children.description: This section details the architectural implementation of Clock (CK) and Track (TRK) lane remapping and repair using differential and pseudo-differential receiver configurations. It addresses how the Physical Layer logic facilitates signal path switching between normal and repair lanes, specifically defining transmitter/receiver tri-state requirements during the detection and recovery phases.

children.title: 4.3.7 Width Degrade in Standard Package Interfaces
children.description: This section details “Width Degrade” for x16 Standard Package interfaces lacking lane‑repair, covering logical lane selection (0‑7 or 8‑15), fault exclusion, link re‑configuration to x8, and associated Valid/Clock/Track repair pathways; it answers how to maintain link resilience when one or more lanes fail, which lanes can be disabled, and the impact on link initialization, training, and data integrity.

children.title: 4.4 Data to Clock Training and Test Modes
children.description: This section details the UCIe architecture for Data to Clock training and link testing, specifically covering infrastructure for LFSR-based pattern generation and comparison. It addresses how compliant dies synchronize transmit and receive patterns to verify signal integrity and interface alignment during initialization or retraining.

children.title: 4.4.1 Scrambling and training pattern generation
children.description: This section details the Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) architecture used for data scrambling and training pattern generation in the UCIe Logical Physical Layer. It specifies the PCIe-compliant polynomial $G(X)=X^{23} + X^{21} + X^{16} + X^8 + X^5 + X^2 + 1$ and provides mandatory lane-dependent seed values to define how chiplets perform de-scrambling and pattern comparison.

children.title: 4.5 Link Initialization and Training
children.description: This section explains UCIe link initialization and training at the module level, defining UCIe Module Partner relationships, Phase Interpolator clock‑phase generation, 8 ms residency timeout rules for all Link‑Training State‑Machine states (including sub‑states), sideband handshakes, stall‑encoded messages, and TRAINERROR→RESET recovery, answering how remote modules negotiate link establishment, enforce state timeouts, select sampling clock phases, and manage error conditions.

children.title: 4.5.1 Link Training Basic Operations
children.description: Section 4.5.1 details the operational procedures for Link Training in high-speed serial interfaces, specifically focusing on transmitter- and receiver-initiated Data-to-Clock point tests and Eye Width Sweeps. This section answers how link alignment and signal integrity are verified during the training phase through systematic clock-to-data timing adjustments.

children.title: 4.5.1.1 Transmitter initiated Data to Clock Point Test
children.description: This section defines the mandatory Sideband (SB) initialization sequences—specifically the SBINIT pattern and {SBINIT done req} message—required to establish UCIe Link training based on Management Transport protocol status. It details failure escalation protocols, transition to RESET for persistent errors, and operational distinctions for sideband-only ports versus those with mainband components.

children.title: 4.5.1.2 Transmitter initiated Data to Clock Eye Width Sweep
children.description: This section details the UCIe physical layer "Transmitter initiated Data to Clock Eye Width Sweep" procedure, defining required transmitter/receiver states and signaling requirements. It clarifies configuration register applications for compliance, debug, and regular operation, specifically addressing how implementations should manage pattern iteration counts and training setup parameters.

children.title: 4.5.1.3 Receiver initiated Data to Clock point test
children.description: This section details the **Receiver-initiated Data to Clock (D to C) eye sweep test** within the UCIe 1.0 specification, covering the sideband signaling handshake, **LFSR (scrambler) reset** procedures, and **mainband pattern comparison** logic. It defines the operational sequence required for UCIe modules to perform precise link training and signal integrity verification between transmitter and receiver partners.

children.title: 4.5.1.4 Receiver initiated Data to Clock Eye Width Sweep
children.description: Section 4.5.1.4 details the protocol for **Receiver-initiated Data to Clock Eye Width Sweep** training, covering the sideband messaging sequence, pattern generation, and comparison logging required to calibrate PI phase alignment. It answers how a UCIe Module orchestrates the communication flow with a partner device to evaluate and store lane-specific eye width data during the Physical Layer initialization process.

children.title: 4.5.2 Link Training with Retimer
children.description: Section 4.5.2 details the UCIe Link Training process involving Retimers, specifically covering independent link initialization, inter-link synchronization, and data rate/width matching protocols. This section answers how UCIe Modules and Retimers coordinate via sideband messaging to achieve compatible configurations across external and chiplet mainband links.

children.title: 4.5.3 Link Training State Machine
children.description: The section details the UCIe Link Training State Machine, covering states such as RESET, SBINIT, MBINIT, MBTRAIN, LINKINIT, ACTIVE

children.description: Reset requirements for the UCIe Logical Physical Layer: minimum 4 ms reset duration, PLL stabilization, power‑supply stability, side

children.title: 4.5.3.2 Sideband Initialization (SBINIT)
children.description: This section details the Sideband Initialization (SBINIT) protocol for UCIe Physical Layer, defining transition requirements, conditional bypass procedures based on SB_MGMT_UP status, and signal state constraints. It clarifies initialization timing, transmitter/receiver power states, and mandatory versus optional configuration steps for Advanced and Standard Packages.
